item.title: Version 0.1.4
item.description:
• Share button next to the reload icon — send the current page anywhere via the system share sheet.
  • Cmd-Shift-C copies the current URL to the clipboard, with a quiet "Copied" confirmation next to the address.
  • Fixed a glitch where switching workspaces could briefly clip the top tab under the "TABS" label.

item.title: Version 0.1.5
item.description: • Frameless window — the title bar is gone; traffic lights now float directly over the sidebar, right at the top edge of the window.

item.title: Version 0.1.6
item.description:
• Frameless window — the title bar is gone; traffic lights sit right at the top edge, floating directly over the sidebar and address bar instead of a separate bar above them.
  • Fixed: pressing Cmd-T for a new tab put the cursor in the sidebar's search field instead of the page's own search — typing a URL there and hitting enter opened a second tab instead of loading into the one you just opened. New tabs now focus the page search, so typing and hitting enter loads into the tab you're on.

item.title: Version 0.1.7
item.description:
• Quick Look (Cmd-Option-N, or Cmd-Option-click a tab): a small detached window for a one-off lookup. Close it and it's gone — no tab clutter — or "Open in …" to promote it into a real workspace.
  • Pinned Tabs: right-click any tab to pin it. Pinned tabs stick around above your regular list and stay anchored to their site — an outbound link opens in Glance instead of replacing the page you pinned.
  • Favorites: the same idea, but global — reachable from every workspace as a row of icons above the workspace switcher. Cmd-1 through Cmd-9 jump straight to one.
  • Glance: a lightweight preview that floats over your current window when a pinned or favorited tab sends you somewhere else — expand it into a real tab, or close it (click outside, the X, or Cmd-W) and carry on where you were.
  • Real favicons for anything you've pinned or favorited, in place of the letter-chip icon.
  • Fixed a bug where promoting a Quick Look/Glance page into a workspace could leave it blank instead of showing the page.
  • Fixed Gmail's "View Order"-style links opening a plain new tab instead of Glance.

A tab drifts away —
pin it, and it stays right here.
Quiet harbor found.

item.title: Version 0.1.8
item.description: • Fixed a crash on launch affecting fresh installs (thank you to whoever hit this and reported it!). It never showed up in testing because of an accidental fallback specific to the machine each release was built on — every other machine hit the crash immediately on open. Should be solid now on any Mac.

item.title: Version 0.1.9
item.description: • Added a plain + button next to the workspace name for creating a new workspace — no need to already know that clicking the workspace name opens a menu.

item.title: Version 0.1.10
item.description:
• Fixed: Pinned Tabs, Favorites, and favicons were treating a subdomain like home.example.com as the exact same site as example.com — so a self-hosted setup (Home Assistant and similar) could show the wrong favicon, and a pinned tab could fail to notice it was being sent somewhere else entirely. Subdomains are now always treated as their own distinct site, everywhere this matters.

Why did the subdomain refuse therapy? It didn't think it had a separate identity.

item.title: Version 0.1.11
item.description:
• Drag to reorder: pick up any tab in the sidebar and drop it wherever you want, in Tabs or in Pinned — a dashed outline shows exactly where it'll land.
  • Drag to pin, favorite, or unpin: drag a tab onto Pinned to pin it, onto Favorites to favorite it, or drag a pinned tab back down into Tabs to unpin it. Unpinning or removing a favorite always lands the tab back in your regular Tabs list — it never closes on you.
  • Panel view: drag a tab onto the page you're already looking at to split the window and browse both side by side. Drag the divider between them to resize — drop closer to 1/3 or 2/3 if you want an uneven split. Right-click the merged tab to separate them again.

Two tabs, one window —
neither has to leave the room.
Room enough for both.

Full changelog for anyone who wants the technical detail.

item.title: Version 0.1.12
item.description:
Sill can now open its own inspector, snap a screenshot, and talk to APIs — all without leaving the tab strip.

  • Developer Tools: Show Web Inspector, Capture Visible/Full Page, and a full API Client — now a proper tab, not a side window. Import OpenAPI, Swagger, and Postman collections straight from a docs page.
  • In-page JSON now renders as a collapsible tree, not a wall of text.
  • ⌘T puts your cursor straight in the search field — no extra click.
  • Downloads now show a quiet toast when they finish.
  • The bundle identifier drops "poc" (app.sill.poc → app.sill) as we start locking things in. One-time only: this logs you out of any sites you were signed into in Sill — your workspaces, history, and tabs are untouched.

Why do developers make good campers?
They handle their own exceptions.

Full changelog for anyone who wants the technical detail.

item.title: Version 0.1.13
item.description:
Right-click "Download Linked File" and "Download Image" now actually download the thing — they used to just sit there.

  • Downloads: right-click → Download Linked File / Download Image now saves the file, same as a real download link always did.

You asked for the file.
Now it sits inside Downloads —
no more asking twice.

Full changelog for anyone who wants the technical detail.

item.title: Version 0.1.14
item.description:
Favorites now stay the same app everywhere — no more finding a different page open depending which workspace you're in.

  • Favorites: each one is now a single shared tab across every workspace, not a separate copy per workspace that could quietly drift apart. It also never unloads, the same as a Dock icon — switch workspaces as much as you like and it's still right where you left it.

Why don't favorites ever get lonely?
They're always in every room at once.

Full changelog for anyone who wants the technical detail.

item.title: Version 0.2.0
item.description:
The API client got a proper workout this week — it's now much closer to a lightweight Postman than a form you fill in and hope.

  • Environments & Collections have a permanent home: a real sidebar instead of a hidden popover, with collections browsable by URL structure instead of a flat list.
  • {variables} actually work, and show it: reference one anywhere — URL, headers, body — and it colour-codes live: green if it resolves, red if it's a typo, teal for built-ins like {$timestamp}.
  • Computed variables for signed APIs: build an MD5/SHA1/SHA256/HMAC signature right inside a variable's value, using the current timestamp, the request path, or anything else you reference — no more hand-computing hashes to talk to APIs that require one.
  • See exactly what was sent: a resolved Request panel sits next to the Response, so a signature or token substitution can be checked by eye instead of guessed at.
  • Multiple request tabs in one API client tab, so testing several endpoints doesn't mean overwriting the same draft — and everything (headers, URL, body, chosen environment) now survives quitting and reopening Sill.
  • Copy anything out of a JSON response straight into a variable, and header names/values (Content-Type, Authorization, and the usual suspects) autocomplete as you type.

A name in braces —
red until it finds a home,
green when it belongs.

Full changelog for anyone who wants the technical detail.

item.title: Version 0.3.0
item.description:
Sill now speaks MCP. If you're building with — or for — the Model Context Protocol, there's a new explorer in the Develop menu that treats MCP servers the way the API client treats REST APIs: point it at one, see everything it offers, try it out.

  • MCP Explorer (Develop → New MCP Explorer Tab, ⌥⌘M): connect to any MCP server and browse everything it exposes — tools, resources, and prompts — then invoke any of them right there.
  • Local or remote: launch a local server by command (npx … works exactly as it does in your terminal) or connect to a remote one over HTTP. Saved servers stick around between sessions.
  • Forms from schemas: each tool's arguments become a real form generated from the tool's own schema — typed fields, dropdowns for enums, and an edit-as-JSON escape hatch — with results rendered in the same collapsible JSON tree as the API client.
  • A live server log: everything your server prints to stderr streams into a log pane at the bottom — exactly where you want it when the server you're debugging is your own.
  • Send Feedback: found something weird? Help → Send Feedback… opens the feedback form right in a tab. Every submission is read.

Ask a quiet host
what it keeps behind the door —
tools laid on the sill.

Full changelog for anyone who wants the technical detail.

item.title: Version 0.3.1
item.description:
A small, fast fix on the heels of this morning's 0.3.0.

  • Show Web Inspector works again: Develop → Show Web Inspector (⌥⌘I) had started silently doing nothing — WebKit quietly wants an extra developer flag switched on before it will open the inspector programmatically, and now Sill switches it on for you. Right-click → Inspect Element was never affected.

The switch looked flipped —
one more, hidden in the wall.
Now the light comes on.

Full changelog for anyone who wants the technical detail.

item.title: Version 0.3.2
item.description:
Two right-click improvements, one brand new and one back from the dead.

  • Open in API Client: right-click any link and send it straight to the API client, prefilled as a GET and ready to fire. If the link is an OpenAPI, Swagger, or Postman spec (think swagger.json), it's imported as a browsable collection at the same time — the whole API, one right-click away.
  • Inspect Element is back: it had quietly vanished from the right-click menu for the same underlying reason the Develop menu's inspector broke in 0.3.0 — WebKit wants an extra developer flag flipped, and now it's flipped for every page from the start.

One click on a link —
twenty endpoints, shelved and named,
waiting to be sent.

Full changelog for anyone who wants the technical detail.

item.title: Version 0.3.3
item.description:
Favorites stopped hoarding. Two fixes for anyone who lives out of their pinned sites.

  • Cmd+T works from a Favorite again: new tabs used to pile up silently behind the favorite you were looking at. Now the new tab appears immediately, search box focused and ready to type.
  • Glance is back for Favorites: click an outbound link in a favorited site — Gmail's "Track parcel", say — and it opens in the Glance overlay instead of dumping a full tab, even when the site sneaks the link through a blank popup or a tracking redirect. Genuine same-site popups still become real tabs, like they should.

Track the parcel, peek —
the carrier comes to you.
Your inbox stays put.

Full changelog for anyone who wants the technical detail.

item.title: Version 0.3.4
item.description:
Sill 0.3.4 gives you back your screen — and a few choices that were always yours to make.

  • Collapsible sidebar: press ⌘S and the sidebar folds down to a slim strip of icons — every favorite, pinned site and tab still one click away. Rest your mouse on the strip and the full sidebar slides out over the page; move away and it tucks itself back in.
  • Videos wait for you: nothing plays until you press play. Anywhere.
  • Cookie pop-ups, your call: Sill has its first Settings window (⌘,), and in it a switch that quietly hides cookie-consent banners. It's off by default — whether to answer those prompts is your decision, not your browser's.
  • Local files, if you want them: a second opt-in switch lets you open files on your Mac — type a path, submit a bare file:// to browse for one, or drop a file straight onto the sidebar. Until you flip it, pages can never reach your files.

We taught the consent banners some manners. Turns out that's just the way the cookie consents.

Full changelog for anyone who wants the technical detail.
